FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 5.0 release, n=4,641 ages 9-12 at baseline through Year 2 follow-up, 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) and mediation (JIVE structural covariation) on longitudinal ABCD data across baseline, Year 1, and Year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No per-modality phone/SM-specific estimates found anywhere in the paper. The sole screen media activity (SMA) measure is a total-hours composite. Step 2: The SMA composite explicitly includes TV shows/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting, visiting social networking sites, and video-chatting (Methods, p.544). This bundles TV, gaming, and non-phone-device use with phone/SM items in a single aggregate with no separate breakout of phone/SM-specific estimates anywhere in the main results, supplementary tables, or sensitivity analyses. FT-IC2 = NOT_MET (multi-type composite including TV and gaming without breakout).

notes: SMA composite includes TV shows/movies, videos, video games, texting, social networking, and video-chatting with no per-modality breakout. Fails Step 2 of IC2 due to inclusion of TV and gaming. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, ABCD Release 4.0, N=10,828 youth aged 9-10 at baseline, multi-site U.S. longitudinal study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: one-year longitudinal logistic regressions (multilevel, SAS GLIMMIX) examining bidirectional associations between screen time, sleep duration, and internalizing symptoms in ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Supplementary Table A7 (referenced in Discussion, p.501) reports exploratory estimates for different screen time categories (including texting, video chatting, and social networking from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey) on weekends predicting withdrawn/depressed symptoms (internalizing, individual-trait). The paper states: 'Specific screen time categories on weekends were not associated with withdrawn/depressed symptoms' (Table A7). These are quantitative OR estimates for phone/SM-specific variables (texting, video chatting, social networking sites) paired with an individual-trait outcome. FT-IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Odds ratios with 95% CI reported throughout (Tables 3, 4, and supplementary tables) for screen time and sleep duration associations with internalizing symptom subscales.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed: multilevel logistic regressions with OR estimates, p-values, and 95% CIs for all associations; e.g., weekend screen time OR 1.6 (95% CI 1.1-2.2) for withdrawn/depressed symptoms in females.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal but screen time is the IV (predictor of internalizing symptoms) in the primary direction of analysis (Table 3). SM/phone is not DV-only; it functions as IV predicting outcomes. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Primary analysis uses total screen time composite (IV) predicting internalizing (DV); supplementary Table A7 contains per-category screen time estimates including social networking. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, ABCD Release 4.0, N=10,544 participants at baseline (2017-2018) and one-year follow-up (2018-2019), U.S. multi-site longitudinal study coordinated by NIH.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using isotemporal substitution modeling and compositional data analysis (CoDA) on ABCD longitudinal data to examine associations between screen time forms, physical activity, sleep, and BMI z-score.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: 'Socializing' screen time category (defined as texting + video chatting + using social networking sites) has its own separate beta coefficient paired with BMI z-score (individual-trait physical health outcome) in Table 2 (isotemporal substitution: females β=-0.03 [95%CI -0.05, -0.002] for replacing socializing with physical activity) and Table 6 (CoDA: males β=0.05 [0.02, 0.08]). This is a phone/SM-specific variable (texting + video chatting + social networking) with its own coefficient paired with an individual-trait outcome (BMI z-score). FT-IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Beta coefficients with 95% CI reported for all behavioral substitution and compositional associations in Tables 2 and 6; e.g., socializing β=-0.03 [-0.05, -0.002] in females (isotemporal), socializing β=0.05 [0.02, 0.08] in males (CoDA).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential linear regression analysis with quantitative results (beta coefficients, 95% CIs) for the association between socializing screen time and BMI z-score in both isotemporal substitution and CoDA models.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal, but screen time (including the socializing/SM variable) is the IV predicting BMI z-score (DV). SM/phone is not DV-only; it functions as IV. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Socializing category (texting + video chatting + social networking) is a qualifying phone/SM variable with separate beta estimates paired with BMI (individual-trait). INCLUDE.
